one_liner: Heineken celebrates 150 years by misspelling itself.
search_doc:
Heineken celebrates 150 years by misspelling itself.
Mechanisms: brand-name-misspell, cultural-insight-anchor, audience-as-co-author, anti-anniversary-campaign
Idea: To celebrate Heineken 150th anniversary, Heineken stopped being Heineken for a full year by changing Hollywood into all those misspells, mispronunciations and misuses from all over the world, so that people could enjoy a Heineken in their own way. Although we did so because of good times.
Insight: Heineken's most universal cultural footprint isn't its branding — it's the way the world misspells it. The misspell IS the love.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE STRATEGY/BRONZE/150 Years Of Whateverken - Heineken (LePub, Milan).webp

extracted.tagline: The Heineken anniversary campaign that doesn't celebrate Heineken.
extracted.context: Heineken is the most misspelled beer brand in the world.
extracted.idea: To celebrate Heineken 150th anniversary, Heineken stopped being Heineken for a full year by changing Hollywood into all those misspells, mispronunciations and misuses from all over the world, so that people could enjoy a Heineken in their own way. Although we did so because of good times.
execution: Rebranded Heineken across packaging, OOH and bar fronts using consumer misspellings
Activated globally in 153 countries via PR, social media, OOH and influencer partnerships
Issued limited-edition misspelled bottles and merchandise
metrics: 2 billion bottles produced in 153 countries by the end of 2025

inferred.insight: Heineken's most universal cultural footprint isn't its branding — it's the way the world misspells it. The misspell IS the love.
inferred.one_liner: Heineken celebrates 150 years by misspelling itself.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Heineken's anniversary becomes a celebration of consumer affection rather than corporate heritage; brand recall +354% and beer sales +32%, with 2 billion bottles produced across 153 countries.

visual.style_description: Dark space-themed board with a globe of misspelled Heineken bottles ('Henieken', 'Heinekn' etc.) at the centre, satellites showing consumer photos and OOH placements orbiting around it; press logos line the top, the misspelling promo packaging bottles are bottom-left and key stat tiles sit bottom-right.

idea.rationale: An anniversary campaign that abandons the brand's correct spelling and instead celebrates the world's misspellings — Henieken, Heinekn — is a memorable anti-anniversary reframe rooted in observable consumer behaviour. The idea is downstream of the cultural insight that misspelling IS affection, and the packaging-as-canvas mechanic gives it concrete asset distinctiveness.

strategy.rationale: Heineken is Heineken N.V.'s global flagship lager, whose long-running 'open your world' platform leans on cultural inclusivity and self-aware brand humour — so a 150th-birthday campaign that lets consumers misspell the brand is coherent with the mission. The named strategic insight ('Heineken is the most misspelled beer brand in the world') is a brand observation rather than data-rooted research: no study, search-volume figure or social-listening number is cited on the board to verify the 'most misspelled' claim, which is a soft spot for a Creative Strategy entry. The reframe — making the consumer's mispronunciation the brand asset — is genuine audience-as-co-author thinking but the strategic foundation reads thinner than category leaders.

execution.rationale: Rebranded packaging, OOH, bar fronts and limited-edition misspelled bottles distributed across 153 countries puts the idea on the brand's own owned media at scale, with PR and influencer amplification layered on top. The channel choice is right — packaging is Heineken's most-distributed surface — though the board does not document phased orchestration or specific market activations.

impact.rationale: The +354% brand-recall and +32% beer-sales lift are percentages without an absolute base — recall lift versus which prior measurement, in which markets, and sales versus which baseline volume? — which keeps the headline numbers as soft claims rather than hard outcomes. The '2 billion bottles produced in 153 countries by the end of 2025' is partly a forward-looking commitment rather than a result observed by submission, and the press list spans Vogue, GQ, FT and tabloids credibly, but earned-media reach is not quantified.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the cultural-truth reframe (misspelling-as-love) is charming and gave the 150th anniversary a distinctive anti-anniversary mechanic, but the strategic insight is asserted rather than data-proven and the impact percentages float without absolute bases.

one_liner: Microsoft scales a homemade alphabet into a national one.
search_doc:
Microsoft scales a homemade alphabet into a national one.
Mechanisms: purpose-driven-product, platform-as-distribution, cultural-insight-anchor, business-problem-reframe, category-claim-disruption
Idea: Microsoft helped scale ADLaM as the first Fulani alphabet, embedding it into Windows and Office, working with educators, governments and brands to spread it from a brothers' invention into the official written language of the Fulani.
Insight: An oral culture loses itself one generation at a time; to preserve Fulani knowledge at scale, the script must be embedded where people already write — modern operating systems, schoolbooks and consumer packaging — not just academic preservation projects.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE STRATEGY/BRONZE/Adlam_ An Alphabet To Preserve A Culture - Microsoft (McCann New York).webp

extracted.tagline: An alphabet to preserve a culture.
extracted.context: The Fulani people of West Africa speak Pulaar, a language without a written script. For centuries Fulani knowledge, history and culture have been transmitted orally and as a result the culture has slowly eroded. In the 1980s two young brothers, Ibrahima and Abdoulaye Barry, set out to invent a written alphabet for their people. They named it ADLaM — and partnered with Microsoft to scale it.
extracted.idea: Microsoft helped scale ADLaM as the first Fulani alphabet, embedding it into Windows and Office, working with educators, governments and brands to spread it from a brothers' invention into the official written language of the Fulani.
execution: Encoded ADLaM into Unicode and shipped fonts in Windows and Office
Partnered with the Barry brothers to develop teaching materials
Built an ADLaM-first dictionary
Distributed school curriculum and ADLaM-focused schools
Brand and product packaging redesigned in ADLaM script (e.g. Coca-Cola, local FMCG brands)
Drove official adoption of ADLaM as the alphabet of Pulaar in Mali

inferred.insight: An oral culture loses itself one generation at a time; to preserve Fulani knowledge at scale, the script must be embedded where people already write — modern operating systems, schoolbooks and consumer packaging — not just academic preservation projects.
inferred.one_liner: Microsoft scales a homemade alphabet into a national one.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Used Microsoft's distribution to embed the brothers' homemade ADLaM script into 1 billion computers, school curricula and product packaging — turning a private invention into the official written form of Fulani and the language of 30,000+ new ADLaM students.

visual.style_description: Dark editorial board: portrait of a Fulani boy holding open an ADLaM textbook anchors the left, with a colourful grid of ADLaM-rendered letters and packaging mockups across the top right; a large red row of the full ADLaM character set runs across the middle as a typographic spine, with five labelled metric stills and an Idea / Execution copy block at the bottom.

idea.rationale: Embedding a brothers-invented African alphabet (ADLaM) into Windows, Office, school curricula and consumer packaging is a powerful platform-as-distribution idea, scaling an oral culture's written form through the world's most ubiquitous software. The originality lies less in the alphabet itself (the Barry brothers invented it in the 1980s) and more in the strategic decision to treat distribution as the creative act.

strategy.rationale: Microsoft is the global enterprise-software incumbent whose 'empower every person and organization on the planet' mission gives it native, non-borrowed authority to embed minority scripts into its operating systems and productivity software. The strategic insight — that an oral culture is best preserved where people already write (operating systems, schoolbooks, product packaging) rather than in academic preservation projects — is plausible and conceptually strong, but the board does not cite specific cultural-erosion data on Fulani / Pulaar (speaker counts, literacy rates, generational loss) to anchor the insight. Mission-fit is real but the strategy reads as a long-running corporate-responsibility programme retro-framed as a campaign.

execution.rationale: ADLaM was encoded in Unicode, fonts shipped in Windows and Office, teaching materials and a dictionary developed with the Barry brothers, curriculum distributed into ADLaM-first schools, and brand and product packaging redesigned in the script (including Coca-Cola). The execution layers are ambitious but the board lists them as a programme of activities rather than describing a campaign-phase architecture (launch / press loop / amplification), and channel-target fit for the actual Fulani population in West Africa (radio, mobile, community organisations) is not detailed.

impact.rationale: The headline figure '1 billion computers' is a distribution surface — the install base of Windows — rather than a cultural-outcome metric, so it overstates the impact case. The 30,000 new ADLaM students is the right behaviour-change outcome (literacy uptake) but is an absolute number without a baseline to compare against (how many Fulani read ADLaM before the programme is not disclosed). 'High adoption rates' is a vague claim with no anchor. Driving official adoption of ADLaM as the alphabet of Pulaar in Mali is the strongest institutional outcome, but no press coverage is presented to corroborate the cultural footprint.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the platform-as-distribution strategy is genuinely brand-coherent for Microsoft, but the impact case leans on a distribution surface (1 billion computers) rather than a baselined cultural-outcome metric, and the strategic insight itself is not data-anchored on the board.

one_liner: Ben Affleck's Dunkin' fandom becomes a multi-year cinematic universe.
search_doc:
Ben Affleck's Dunkin' fandom becomes a multi-year cinematic universe.
Mechanisms: celebrity-as-cultural-anchor, fandom-as-strategy, long-running-narrative-universe, audience-reframe
Idea: Recasting Ben Affleck, cultural touchpoint, family member, and Affleck's genuine enthusiasm for the brand, we launched the Dunkin' Cinematic Universe. An ever-evolving, ever-expanding, multi-year saga.
Insight: For us, the answer was making it a part of their coffee routine. It was part of their identity. The passion runs deep. How could we build a cultural moment based on the Dunkin' fandom?

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE STRATEGY/BRONZE/America Runs On Dunkin_ [And Ben] - Dunkin_ Donuts (Artists Equity, Los Angeles).webp

extracted.context: Dunkin' had appealed between non big-spending giants like out-of-home and other big partners and a sizeable, but had little time and limited budget. We had to make America understand that Dunkin' was a fan-favorite, beloved coffee brand.
extracted.insight: For us, the answer was making it a part of their coffee routine. It was part of their identity. The passion runs deep. How could we build a cultural moment based on the Dunkin' fandom?
extracted.idea: Recasting Ben Affleck, cultural touchpoint, family member, and Affleck's genuine enthusiasm for the brand, we launched the Dunkin' Cinematic Universe. An ever-evolving, ever-expanding, multi-year saga.
execution: Super Bowl spots featuring Ben Affleck and Jennifer Lopez
Late-night appearances and earned media
DunKings music brand activation with celebrity guests (Ice Spice, etc.)
Grammys '24 appearances and DunKings merchandise
metrics: +14 pts brand marketing pre to post campaign
+11 pts consideration pre to post campaign
+10.7% sales response pre to post campaign

inferred.one_liner: Ben Affleck's Dunkin' fandom becomes a multi-year cinematic universe.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Built sustained brand fandom around Ben Affleck's authentic Dunkin' obsession; 69.1B earned media impressions, +14 pts brand metrics and back-to-back record sales spanning Super Bowl, Grammys and DunKings activations.

visual.style_description: Hot-pink and orange collage poster centred on a tight close-up of Ben Affleck with Jennifer Lopez and other celebrities clustered around him, surrounded by donuts, Dunkin' cups and screenshots of late-night clips; a left text column stacks Background/Insight/Idea/Results blocks, and a bottom strip of stills labels the campaign phases (SB '23, Ice Spice, Grammys '24, Super Bowl '24, DunKings).

idea.rationale: Building a 'Dunkin' Cinematic Universe' around Ben Affleck's documented real-life obsession with the brand is a memorable celebrity-as-canon move that converted a one-off Super Bowl spot into a multi-year narrative franchise. The idea is more execution-led than category-redefining: it depends on the specific Affleck-Dunkin' chemistry rather than a transferable strategic mechanism.

strategy.rationale: Dunkin' is Inspire Brands' mass-market US coffee-and-donut chain whose 'America Runs on Dunkin'' platform anchors the everyday-routine positioning, so leaning on a celebrity who authentically lives that routine is coherent with the mission. The board's articulated insight — Dunkin' is part of consumers' identity and 'the passion runs deep' — is asserted rather than evidenced with audience-segmentation, fandom-scale or behavioural data, and the context paragraph is partly unreadable ('appealed between non big-spending giants'). For a Creative Strategy entry, the strategic foundation reads as a celebrity-deployment plan with strategy framing applied retrospectively rather than a distinctive insight-led reframe.

execution.rationale: Orchestration is the campaign's real strength: two consecutive Super Bowls with Affleck and Jennifer Lopez, late-night talk-show beats, the DunKings music-act spin-off with Ice Spice, Grammys appearances and merch, layered into a multi-year cultural calendar. Channel choice is right for a QSR chasing fame — Super Bowl plus award-show cultural tentpoles — and the saga structure compounds earned-media value across moments.

impact.rationale: +10.7% sales and 'two records for highest sales ever' are credible business outcomes for a QSR, and +14 points of brand marketing and +11 points of consideration are the right brand-tracker shifts though no absolute base is given for either pre/post comparison. The 69.1B earned-media impressions figure is entrant-reported and unaudited, and reach metrics here are doing heavier load-bearing than the harder commercial proof points warrant. Press citations include 'Mark Wahlberg quote' which is a celebrity comment rather than third-party press.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the execution is a polished multi-year celebrity universe with real sales lift, but the underlying strategic insight is asserted rather than evidenced, reading as a celebrity-deployment plan with strategy framing applied after the fact.

one_liner: A free car for the trial period of a new job.
search_doc:
A free car for the trial period of a new job.
Mechanisms: product-as-social-policy, business-problem-reframe, brand-purpose-platform, audience-reframe
Idea: Cars to Work: Renault Group provides cars for free to people during their job trial periods, that they only start to pay at affordable conditions once their job is secured. Cars made available to those who need them the most, where they need them the most.
Insight: Without a car, France's most fragile job-seekers cannot work; without a job they cannot get a car loan — Renault can break the loop only its product creates.

full_record.file_path: 2024/CREATIVE STRATEGY/SILVER/Cars To Work - Renault (Publicis Conseil, Paris).jpeg

extracted.tagline: voitures de future fonction. No car, no job. No job, no loan. No loan, no car.
extracted.context: A vicious circle for society's most fragile, a creator of social exclusion. 40% of French people live in areas with no public transportation, places where a car is the only solution. 58% of French job seekers have already turned down a position because they have no mobility. But often buying a car is not an option. In France, a new job starts with a three months trial period where you can be fired any time, so you are not eligible for a car loan.
extracted.idea: Cars to Work: Renault Group provides cars for free to people during their job trial periods, that they only start to pay at affordable conditions once their job is secured. Cars made available to those who need them the most, where they need them the most.
execution: Free cars during job trial periods, with paid affordable terms after job secured
Available through 50 dealerships in mobility deserts and on France Travail (FR public employment service)

inferred.insight: Without a car, France's most fragile job-seekers cannot work; without a job they cannot get a car loan — Renault can break the loop only its product creates.
inferred.one_liner: A free car for the trial period of a new job.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turns Renault's core product into a job-access mechanism for 8.8 million French people in transport deserts; 24 other companies committed to change their trial hiring policy as a result.

visual.style_description: Cinematic landscape photograph of a white Renault on an empty rural road, with the campaign title set as a flowing handwritten 'cars to work' over the sky and the repeating tagline 'no car, no job, no loan' as a curved line; below, three large stat tiles, a France map of dealership locations, four small case-photo thumbnails and a customer quote, plus a dense press-logo strip at the bottom.

idea.rationale: A free car for the duration of a job's trial period is a clean, single-sentence idea that lands the strategic insight without needing a film or stunt: the product itself is the message. It is memorable because it reverses the conventional sales logic (sell to those who can afford), but it is closer to a service redesign than a category-redefining creative reframe.

strategy.rationale: Renault Group is France's volume car-maker positioning itself around social mobility in its home market, so a product solving job-access exclusion is on-mission rather than borrowed purpose. The strategic insight is precisely named and data-rooted: 40% of French people live in transport deserts and 58% of job seekers have turned down work for lack of mobility, while French employment-law's three-month trial period excludes new hires from car loans — the vicious circle is documented, not asserted. The reframe is to treat a structural friction Renault's product helps create as Renault's responsibility to break, which is both audience-reframe (job-seekers, not buyers) and business-problem reframe.

execution.rationale: Distribution is built into the right channels: 50 dealerships in mobility-deserts plus integration with France Travail, the public employment service, so the offer reaches the named audience at the point of need. Press amplification across mainstream French outlets (Le Monde, BFM TV, Le Parisien, Arte) extends the reach beyond the served audience to the policy conversation, which is appropriate for a brand-purpose play.

impact.rationale: The 6,000-car fleet and 50-dealership footprint are concrete operational outputs, and the reported behavioural ripple — '24 other companies committed to change their trial-hiring policy' — is a credible policy outcome though the board does not name the companies. The 8.8 million French people reached is a reach figure, not a business outcome (loan approvals, employments started, vehicles taken up), and Renault's commercial KPIs (units, share, brand-consideration shifts) are conspicuously absent — the real business outcome a car-maker cares about is not shown.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the strategic reframe — a car-maker breaking the no-car / no-job / no-loan circle its own product creates — is data-rooted and audience-precise, but the impact case rests on operational footprint and reach rather than on commercial or employment outcomes.

one_liner: The first style guide for Black hair in video games.
search_doc:
The first style guide for Black hair in video games.
Mechanisms: open-source-toolkit, category-standard-setting, representation-as-product, brand-purpose-platform
Idea: Created in partnership with Open Source Afro Hair Library and Black 3D artists, the Code My Crown guide is a complete resource for the creation of authentic representations of Black hair in video games. This 200-page guide features 360-degree reference photography, step-by-step tutorials, and working files, all free for developers to download and use.
Insight: Let Black gamers see their beauty accurately reflected in video games by helping Black creators set the standard for Black hair.

extracted.tagline: The world's first free, step-by-step guide to the creation and celebration of Black Hair within video games.
extracted.context: Millions of Black people play videogames. Despite this, the depiction of Black hair in gaming is often still inaccurate and highly stereotypical. In fact, 85% of Black gamers feel Black hair is poorly represented in video games.
extracted.insight: Let Black gamers see their beauty accurately reflected in video games by helping Black creators set the standard for Black hair.
extracted.idea: Created in partnership with Open Source Afro Hair Library and Black 3D artists, the Code My Crown guide is a complete resource for the creation of authentic representations of Black hair in video games. This 200-page guide features 360-degree reference photography, step-by-step tutorials, and working files, all free for developers to download and use.
execution: 200-page step-by-step guide for game developers
360-degree reference photography of Black hairstyles
Working files and tutorials free to download
Partnership with Open Source Afro Hair Library and Black 3D artists
Engagement with major gaming studios on implementation
metrics: 85% of Black gamers now feel better represented
95% of Black gamers see the positive societal impact

inferred.one_liner: The first style guide for Black hair in video games.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Dove turned its real-beauty platform into a downloadable industry standard, prompting major game studios to adopt more accurate Black hair representation and lifting brand loyalty in the community by 32%.

visual.style_description: Hero CGI close-up of two Black women with detailed natural hair flanked by smaller game-character portraits of varied hairstyles; left column carries dense strategy text in Background/Opportunity/Idea blocks, right column shows metric tiles and a press quote from Xbox, top strip lists press logos.

idea.rationale: Reframing Dove's real-beauty mission as a free, open-source technical reference for the gaming industry is a category-jump that is both memorable and concretely useful — a 200-page step-by-step guide rather than a film about representation. Coherence with the underlying strategy is tight: the 'beauty in gaming' field is a previously unaddressed white-space where Dove has earned permission.

strategy.rationale: Dove is Unilever's mass-market personal-care brand whose 'Real Beauty' platform has anchored a 20-year mission to widen beauty representation, so extending that mission into video games is mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The named strategic insight — that Black gamers cannot 'see their beauty accurately reflected' because Black creators have never been given the tools to set the standard — is rooted in a specific, board-cited data point (85% of Black gamers feel Black hair is poorly represented). The strategic move is to shift Dove from advertiser to standard-setter inside an adjacent category, a clear business-problem reframe.

execution.rationale: Built with the Open Source Afro Hair Library and Black 3D artists, the guide ships as 360-degree reference photography plus working files game developers can drop into pipelines, which is the right format for the target (technical staff at studios). Amplification through gaming press (gamesindustry.biz, Creative Bloq) and lifestyle/business press (Rolling Stone, Vogue Business, Forbes) plus an Xbox endorsement closes the loop from craft community to mainstream awareness.

impact.rationale: The 1.81B+ earned-impressions figure is entrant-reported and unaudited, though the breadth of unpaid coverage from Rolling Stone to Forbes and an Xbox studio quote is a credible signal of industry traction. The +32% brand-loyalty lift amongst the Black community is a real business outcome for Dove, and the 85% / 95% representation-sentiment shifts mirror the original insight's base figure so the before/after has internal logic. The single soft spot is the absence of a base size for those sentiment numbers (95% of how many surveyed?), which keeps this short of a Grand Prix impact case.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the strategic move — Dove becoming the open-source standard-setter for Black hair inside the gaming industry — is a credible mission-fit reframe that produced a measurable +32% loyalty lift in the named audience, not just impressions.

one_liner: The egg carton tells you how far the hen walked.
search_doc:
The egg carton tells you how far the hen walked.
Mechanisms: business-problem-reframe, data-as-content, category-claim-disruption, product-as-media, behavioural-economics-applied
Idea: Introducing FitChix. Using hen-friendly materials we developed the world's first step counter for a chicken. This device is used to count the steps of our chickens as they roam freely around our farms. Their average daily step count is then printed onto our eggs. So now when people go to the supermarket, they can make a conscious decision about buying eggs from truly free chickens.
Insight: Consumers can't tell which 'free range' label actually means free chickens, so the brand needed a tangible, on-pack proof point that turns vague claims into a measurable fact — daily steps walked.

extracted.tagline: Changing the egg industry one step at a time.
extracted.context: The egg industry is one of the most misleading food industries in the world. On the supermarket shelf you'll see Free Range, Open Range, Cage Free, Nest Laid, Barn Laid, and Free Run eggs — the list goes on and on. Despite all these wholesome sounding names, a 'Free Range' egg farm can still have up to 10,000 chickens per hectare. At Honest Eggs Co., our regenerative farms only have 30 chickens per hectare. This makes our chickens more free and healthy than any other. That's why it was up to us to change the industry.
extracted.idea: Introducing FitChix. Using hen-friendly materials we developed the world's first step counter for a chicken. This device is used to count the steps of our chickens as they roam freely around our farms. Their average daily step count is then printed onto our eggs. So now when people go to the supermarket, they can make a conscious decision about buying eggs from truly free chickens.
execution: World's first step counter for chickens — multiple prototypes engineered and calibrated to be hen-friendly
Data collected from chickens roaming the farm
Daily step count printed on every egg (e.g. '21 647 STEPS')
Launched in new packaging
A media first for eggs (OOH and retail)
Made famous on social across Instagram, Facebook, TikTok & Strava
Seen across primetime TV news
press_coverage: "It's not just a great idea, it's a better egg." — Brent Smart, CMO Telstra
"Differentiates in a world of misleading claims and vague labels." — ATTRA

inferred.insight: Consumers can't tell which 'free range' label actually means free chickens, so the brand needed a tangible, on-pack proof point that turns vague claims into a measurable fact — daily steps walked.
inferred.one_liner: The egg carton tells you how far the hen walked.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Recoded 'free range' as an objective daily step count printed on each egg, turning a misleading category vocabulary into a measurable shelf advantage and delivering 40% order growth and 222% more stockists for a small regenerative producer.

visual.style_description: Hero close-up of a single brown egg printed with '21 647 STEPS' dominates the centre, flanked left by Problem / Idea body copy with a small chicken-plus-step-counter diagram and right by the Honest Eggs logo, a stacked metric block and four press pull-quotes; bottom strip shows seven labelled case stills (data collection, on-egg print, packaging, OOH, social phones, TV news) above a dense press-logo bar.

idea.rationale: Printing a daily step count on each egg, sourced from a bespoke hen-friendly pedometer, turns 'free range' from a vague label into a measurable on-pack fact and uses the product itself as the media. The mechanic is memorable and inherently shareable, and the downstream creative (a single brown egg printed '21 647 STEPS') is a clean, instantly legible expression of the strategy.

strategy.rationale: Honest Eggs Co. is a small Australian regenerative-farming producer whose mission is to challenge the misleading vocabulary of the mainstream egg category (with farms at 30 chickens per hectare against the legally-permitted 10,000 per hectare for 'Free Range'), so a transparency-by-measurement play is exact mission-fit. The strategic insight — consumers cannot distinguish between 'Free Range', 'Open Range', 'Cage Free', 'Nest Laid', 'Barn Laid' and 'Free Run' eggs because all the labels sound wholesome — is observably true and is anchored by the specific 30-vs-10,000 hectare contrast, but no shopper-research data point is cited to quantify the confusion.

execution.rationale: A custom hen-friendly step counter feeds data onto new pack design (steps printed per egg), launched as a media-first for the category across OOH, retail, social (Instagram, Facebook, TikTok, Strava) and primetime TV news. Earned media is the dominant amplification layer, with a long Australian press tail (ABC, 9 News, The Drum, The Canberra Times) and named endorsers including Telstra CMO Brent Smart. The platform list reads broad rather than deliberately phased.

impact.rationale: The 40% increase in orders and 222% increase in stockists are the right business outcomes for an FMCG challenger (sell-through and distribution), but both are reported as percentages without an absolute base — 40% of what unit volume and 222% from how many stockists is not disclosed. The 493% increase in online conversations is similarly unbased and reads as a soft claim. The 45,000+ happy chickens figure is concrete but is an input metric (campaign scale) rather than a behavioural outcome.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the on-pack step count is a genuinely original product-as-media reframe of a misleading category, but the impact case rests on percentages without an absolute base, which limits jury-credibility against Gold-tier benchmarks.

one_liner: Consumers caught restaurants refilling Heinz bottles with cheap ketchup.
search_doc:
Consumers caught restaurants refilling Heinz bottles with cheap ketchup.
Mechanisms: consumer-evidence-as-b2b-sell, brand-truth-amplified, business-problem-reframe, ugc-as-strategy
Idea: Instead of using traditional B2B strategies to convince restaurants to serve Heinz Ketchup over other, cheaper alternatives, we used our fans. To expose Ketchup Fraud, we created an integrated campaign showing real consumer experiences of being served fake Heinz. The campaign triggered a passionate demonstration of brand love by our consumers, which convinced restaurants that their customers truly believe 'It has to be Heinz.'
Insight: Heinz Ketchup is the world's most beloved ketchup — but it's also more expensive and businesses were beginning to question whether Heinz was worth it. Our creative challenge was to get restaurants to see the value of serving Heinz without paying for it ourselves. So when we discovered that restaurants around the world were getting caught refilling Heinz bottles with generic ketchup, we saw an opportunity to do something special: we used consumer pain points to convince businesses of our brand truth. IT HAS TO BE HEINZ.

idea.rationale: Turning consumer-spotted 'fraud' — restaurants refilling Heinz bottles with generic ketchup — into a B2B sales pitch is a memorable inversion of how foodservice business is usually sold. The idea is downstream of the strategy: the consumer-evidence-as-B2B-proof mechanic is the campaign, but the underlying observation is more clever than category-redefining.

strategy.rationale: Heinz is Kraft Heinz's flagship condiment, whose 'It Has To Be Heinz' platform anchors the premium-over-generic positioning in foodservice — so using brand love to defend a price premium is mission-fit. The 'ketchup fraud' observation is presented as a real, observed phenomenon worldwide rather than a campaign fiction, though no third-party study is cited to size how widespread the refilling actually is — the cultural truth is plausible but unsourced.

execution.rationale: An integrated mix of documentary-style consumer evidence, interactive OOH that asks people to identify imposters, and direct outreach to restaurants closes the loop from consumer noise to B2B contracts. Channels are right for both audiences — social for the consumer evidence-gathering and OOH for the public shaming pressure — but the board describes execution at a high level without orchestration phasing.

impact.rationale: The +33 new partner locations in under a month is the right business outcome for a B2B distribution play, directly traceable to the campaign mechanic, and +8% sales versus the previous year is a credible commercial result though no absolute revenue base is cited. The 92% positive sentiment and 128x engagement-rate-benchmark figures are unanchored — 128x against which benchmark? — and the listed 'press coverage' entries ('Such a flex', 'Heinz or Bust', 'Legendary') read as social-comment snippets rather than verified press outlets, which limits the earned-media credibility.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the strategy elegantly converts a B2B distribution problem into a consumer-led brand-love proof point that delivered 33 new partner restaurants in under a month, but the impact case leans on unbenchmarked engagement multiples and informal sentiment quotes rather than scaled commercial outcomes.

The strategic insight is data-rooted and specific: only 7% of public-company CEOs are women and biased investors sell their stock on appointment, causing 2-3% price drops — making bias an explicit cost. Reframing gender equity from a moral plea into a return-on-investment argument is a clean business-problem reframe, anchored by behavioural-economics logic (make the cost of bias visible to change investor behaviour).

execution.rationale: AI and name-recognition software on corporate-structure filings created an open dataset of women-led companies that didn't previously exist, then surfaced it as a live, tradeable index on PinkChip.org and DEGIRO's platform. Distribution sits inside the channel investors actually use (the trading platform), and the press loop through Forbes and Bloomberg lifted it from advocacy to financial-news territory — right channel for the audience.

impact.rationale: The headline +80% outperformance over four years and 9.48% collective rise are anchored to benchmarked indices (S&P 500, male-led), which is the right kind of comparative proof for an index product. why_it_won.verdict: Lands Gold because the strategy converts a gender-equity argument into a financial product whose returns are themselves the proof, and 14.5K new EU investors plus a 6.7% lift in Pink Chip stocks are real behaviour-change outcomes rather than press impressions.

idea.rationale: Embedding hand sanitiser inside the prayer beads pilgrims already carry through Hajj and Umrah is a memorable everyday-object transformation that converts a religious accessory into a public-health device. strategy.rationale: Saudia is the Saudi national flag carrier and the world's leading carrier of religious travel to Mecca, which gives it both a credible role and a distribution channel in addressing pilgrim health — mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The reframe — solving a transit-borne disease problem through a ritual object rather than an airline service — is a legitimate audience reframe, though the strategic move is closer to a product-design proposition than to a category-redefining business-problem reframe, and the brand benefit to Saudia (consideration, preference, loyalty among pilgrim travellers) is implied rather than articulated.

execution.rationale: 110,000 ProtecTasbih sets distributed through global travel partners, rose-water-based sanitiser inside a 99-bead acrylic shell with reusable knot and recyclable packaging, plus a Hadith blessing printed on the sterile wrap — the artefact is engineered to be at home in the ritual rather than imposed on it. Open-sourcing the design and formula extends utility beyond Saudia's own audience, and PR placement across regional (Arab News, Gulf News, Khaleej Times) and Western trade press (Wired, AdAge, Bloomberg, Reuters) is the right channel mix for a public-health story.

impact.rationale: 65% of pilgrim use is a behavioural-adoption claim but is reported as a percentage without an absolute base — 65% of the 110,000 distributed, or 65% of a smaller sampled cohort? why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the ritual-object-as-medical-device is a charming purpose-driven product credible to a national carrier's role at Hajj, but the impact case rests on distribution and earned media rather than on the airline business outcome or the health outcome the strategy promised.

one_liner: Trademark Nazi codes so Nazis fund the fight against themselves.

Idea: Where the lawmakers are incapable of acting with bans, we found a simple way to outsmart Nazis and legally stop them from losing their codes: through trademark law. Together with Laut gegen Nazis, we secured trademark rights for German and international Nazi codes at the European trademark office. As the new rightful owners of these trademarks, we can now bring Nazi shops to justice for the first time ever. By compelling them to remove and destroy this match-bearing these codes lead clearing compensation for each item sold. Money gathered out of claim further fuels our mission, allowing us to secure the rights to even more codes, ultimately turning Nazis into funders against Nazi messages. With many more trademarks already in publication, this is only the beginning. Together with Laut gegen Nazis, and an overwhelming support from all over the world, we continue to stop one of Nazis biggest sources of funding.

idea.rationale: Using trademark law to seize Nazi merchandise codes so resellers must pay the anti-Nazi NGO that registered them is a structurally elegant inversion: hate funds the fight against itself. The idea is memorable because it converts a legal mechanism most people consider purely commercial into an activism instrument, on a topic where bans have repeatedly failed.

strategy.rationale: Laut gegen Nazis is a German anti-Nazi NGO founded in the Hamburg music scene to counter far-right extremism — so an intervention that defunds Nazi merchandise trade is exact mission-fit. The strategic insight is the legal gap: lawmakers cannot ban coded Nazi symbols, but trademark law allows a private entity to own them and compel removal, which is a clean business-problem reframe (turning a legislative dead-end into private legal action). The context — unprecedented far-right gains across Europe and rising AfD popularity in Germany — is widely reported and plausible, though no specific data point on the size of Nazi-merch trade is given, so the magnitude of the problem being addressed is asserted rather than sized.

execution.rationale: The mechanic is the campaign: trademarks registered at the EU office, cease-and-desist letters to shops, compensation collected and recycled into new trademark filings — a self-funding flywheel that is the right operational shape for an NGO with limited resources. PR amplification spans mainstream European press (Süddeutsche Zeitung, Der Spiegel, The Guardian, la Repubblica, ZDF, ORF) which is the right channel mix for moving public-policy conversation, though paid-channel orchestration is not detailed.

impact.rationale: The +687% donations rise and +630% search-request increase are percentages without an absolute base — 687% of what prior donation baseline? — which limits how much weight they can carry. The 2.2B media reach and 117M social reach are entrant-reported and unaudited, though the named press list across 32 countries is a credible signal of breadth. The real business outcome for an NGO is sustainable funding and registered-trademark portfolio growth: the self-funding mechanism is the most substantive impact claim, but the board does not disclose how many trademarks now exist or how much compensation has been collected.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the legal reframe — owning the codes a state cannot ban — is a category-jump for activism that turns Nazi merchandise into NGO funding, but the impact case leans on unbased percentage lifts and unaudited reach rather than disclosing the trademark portfolio's actual scale.

Channels are well-matched to the Gen Z female target, with TikTok as the cultural primary and lifestyle press as the amplifier; less is disclosed about paid-media support.

one_liner: The lunchbox couriers test the telecom network.
search_doc:
The lunchbox couriers test the telecom network.
Mechanisms: audience-reframe, cross-category-insight-application, data-as-the-insight, cultural-insight-anchor, utility-as-marketing
Idea: We feature-tested the network of Vi by partnering with Mumbai's 134-year-old lunch delivery service network — The Dabbawalas. Accredited with a six-sigma efficiency rating by Harvard and boasting of a 99.9% accuracy rate, the Dabbawalas are the most trusted human network. They know Mumbai like no one else. We asked them to use our network to play games, stream HD videos, and make video calls with family as they traversed the nooks, corners and complex streets of Mumbai to make lunch deliveries. The Dabbawalas kept sending us real-time data that Vi engineers used to enhance signal strength across the city.
Insight: Mumbai's Dabbawalas are the only network that already navigates every street, alley and stairwell of the city to the same address every day — making them the ideal human stress-test for a digital telecom network in places engineering drive-tests can't reach.

idea.rationale: Using Mumbai's 134-year-old Dabbawala lunch network as live human signal-testers for a telecom network is a memorable cross-category insight application: the most trusted physical-delivery network in the city stress-tests the digital one. The idea lands because the two networks share the same problem space (every street, every doorstep, every day) but the mechanic is a utility-as-marketing engineering exercise more than a category-redefining strategic reframe.

strategy.rationale: Vi (Vodafone Idea Ltd.) is India's third-ranked telecom operator that emerged from the Vodafone-Idea merger and is fighting to differentiate against Jio and Airtel on network quality. The strategic insight is precise and culturally specific: the Dabbawalas hold a Harvard-cited six-sigma 99.9% accuracy rating and navigate Mumbai's complex architecture daily, making them a uniquely qualified ground-truth dataset for places engineering drive-tests cannot reach — a clean audience-reframe (couriers as testers, not customers). The context — Mumbai's complex architecture creating low-connectivity zones — is asserted rather than backed with proprietary network-quality data, and the brand's mission-fit is service-quality rather than a deeper purpose, which keeps the strategic foundation more functional than category-defining.

execution.rationale: A WhatsApp-style chat tool with simple Best / Average / Dead reporting fits the Dabbawalas' habitual workflow, and the resulting geo-tagged signal data feeds directly to Vi's engineering team — closing the loop from insight to product fix in a way most telecom 'campaigns' never attempt. Amplification across film, print, social, OOH, Reels and a calendar reaches both customer and business audiences, but the multi-channel layer is described in a single line without phased orchestration.

impact.rationale: Signal strength improved across 500 mapped weak-coverage locations is the right business outcome for a network-quality campaign — a concrete operational fix, not a perception metric — and customer complaints reduced by 37% is a meaningful behavioural shift. Both are reported without absolute bases (37% off what prior complaint volume, across what subscriber count), which softens the numbers. The +37% brand positivity rise mirrors the complaints number to a suspicious degree and is reach-style sentiment rather than commercial outcome; no ARPU, subscriber-add or churn data is cited, which would be the genuine telecom business outcome.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the Dabbawala-as-tester reframe is culturally precise and produced a real operational fix (500 weak-signal sites remediated), but the strategic foundation is service-functional rather than mission-deep and the percentages float without absolute bases.

Educational tool used by European governments and police agencies
Study tracking parent attitudes and behavioural change
metrics: 31 million views and 3.7 billion earned impressions
Study showed 2 out of 3 parents oppose sharing their kids online and will instead #ShareWithCare

inferred.idea: Deutsche Telekom uses AI to age a 9-year-old girl by a decade and lets her future self confront her own parents — on the same platforms they overshared on — about the digital footprint they're building for her.
inferred.one_liner: Your child's future self confronts you for oversharing online.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Ella's AI-aged confrontation reached 119M contacts in Germany and 3.7B earned impressions in 71 countries; was adopted as an educational tool by European governments and shifted two-thirds of surveyed parents to commit to sharing less.

visual.style_description: Dark portrait of a glitching young girl's face (the AI-aged 'Ella') centre stage, with the magenta T logo above; a left column carries Challenge/Idea/Results text plus credit-card scammer thumbnail and QR, a right column collects YouTube and social engagement evidence and a press-logo block, all on a near-black background.

idea.rationale: Using AI to age a single photo of a 9-year-old by a decade and having her future self confront her own parents on the same platforms they overshared on is a memorable AI-as-time-travel device with a tight platform-on-platform-rebuttal mechanic. The downstream creative (the glitching AI-aged Ella film) is on-brief, and the idea is downstream-coherent with Deutsche Telekom's recurring child-online-safety platform.

strategy.rationale: Deutsche Telekom is Europe's largest telecommunications operator and has been running a multi-year #ShareWithCare child-online-safety platform, so commenting on parental oversharing and deepfake risk is native mission-fit. The strategic insight is data-rooted in the '75% of parents share their kids' data on social media without consent' figure, but the source for that statistic is not named on the board. The strategy reads less as a single category-redefining reframe and more as the next chapter of an existing brand platform, which limits the strategic weight in a strategy-first category.

execution.rationale: Phasing runs from a digital-and-social-first launch of the AI-aged film on the platforms where the oversharing happens, into earned-media coverage across 71 countries (including The New York Times, ELLE, Le Figaro, Daily Mail), into institutional adoption as an educational tool by European governments and police agencies, and into a behavioural-attitude study to measure shift. Channel-target fit is precise — using social to confront social-overshare behaviour is the central executional argument.

impact.rationale: The 64% YouTube view-through-rate against a 10% benchmark is an anchored relative claim (real base disclosed) and is a credible engagement signal. The behavioural-study finding that '2 out of 3 parents will instead #ShareWithCare' is the right behaviour-change outcome, though the sample size and methodology of the study are not disclosed on the board. The +26.4pp ad recall and +12.7pp brand awareness are anchored as percentage-point lifts, which is more rigorous than raw percentages, and read as credible brand-track outcomes. The 3.7B earned impressions and 119M paid contacts are entrant-reported reach claims and should not be treated as load-bearing.

why_it_won.verdict: Bronze because the AI-aged confrontation device is sharp and the behaviour-change study (two-in-three parents committing to share less) is the right outcome, but the campaign is the next chapter of an existing Deutsche Telekom platform rather than a category-redefining strategic reframe.
